title: RBA left with no wiggle room on rates
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Business RBA left with no wiggle room on rates The Australian DAVID ROGERS, EXCHANGE 4 May 2026 893 words English AUSTLN 15 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. The Reserve Bank is likely to present the monetary policy board with a range of scenarios on global energy prices at its meeting this week, amid heightened uncertainty caused by the Iran war. The board may formally assess the implications for economic growth and inflation of a scenario where energy prices fall more rapidly than implied by futures curves, and also an option where energy prices rise sharply and remain elevated for a prolonged period. The RBA used scenario analysis during the tariff war 12 months ago to assess the range of plausible economic outcomes. But with inflation expectations spiking and consumer confidence tanking, there’s no way the board can claim that policy is “well placed” to deal with the stagflationary scenario that’s now unfolding. HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham says it’s a good approach for central banks in highly uncertain times. In his view, the main risk is “a longer period of closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which leads to more supply chain disruptions, which means weaker growth, higher inflation”. The key thing to watch for is what the RBA says about how it is likely to react. Hints at whether it’s more focused on inflation or growth risks will be closely watched by markets. As the Bank of England’s monetary policy committee said last week, monetary policy needs to “balance the costs of leaning too little against second-round effects” and the “costs of responding too much” and pushing the economy into recession. It warned that “the fact that this shock is happening after several years of above-target inflation could boost the strength of second-round effects”. This could also be the case in Australia. In its latest outlook, the International Monetary Fund considered two different “downside scenarios”. Its “adverse” scenario is based on the Iran war ending in 2026. Its “severe” scenario contemplates a “larger and more persistent shock” that extends into next year. The “adverse” scenario has a significant impact in reducing global economic growth by 0.8 percentage points to 2.3 per cent, and by increasing inflation by 1.5 percentage points in 2026. More troubling, though, is the “severe” scenario, which dramatically reduces global growth in 2026 and 2027 and raises inflation by about 2 percentage points for both years. Bloxham says it’s a “very difficult spot” for policy makers at the moment, particularly for central bankers, because the shock that’s being delivered is a stagflationary shock. “It weakens growth and lifts inflation at the same time.” When you’re a central bank with a dual mandate like the RBA, to keep inflation on target but also stay close to full employment, “it’s pushing you away from both of your objectives”, he says. No doubt the balance of risk in Australia is skewed toward “leaning too little” against inflation, which is why the RBA has rushed to lift interest rates twice this year while other central banks haven’t. While inflation is bubbling up everywhere due to the unprecedented closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the ensuing supply shock for oil, gas, fertiliser and other industrial materials, inflation rates in other developed nations are now hitting the low threes. Australia’s inflation rate hit 4.6 per cent last month, even before the full impact of second-round price increases show up in official data. Bloxham says the evidence is clear – every major central bank met last week but none lifted rates. Yet there is an expectation that the RBA will be lifting interest rates again this week. “To me, that tells us that we are less well placed than all of these other countries, because their central banks have felt that they have time on their hands,” he says. “They can afford to sit still and wait and see, and our central bank doesn’t have that option.” Australia already has “an economy which is already operating beyond its full capacity, with an unemployment rate that’s already below full employment. So we’ve already got a hot economy, an economy that’s too hot, and we’re now getting another inflationary shock.” As of last week, money market pricing implied a 75 per cent chance of the RBA lifting the cash rate from 4.1 per cent to 4.35 per cent on Tuesday. A total 60 basis points of hikes are expected this year, pushing standard variable mortgage rates up to about 7.25 per cent. But soaring prices and interest rates will soon hit economic growth. Bloxham says the RBA should hike to “credibly argue that inflation will return to target over a reasonable horizon” but the Middle East crisis is a “negative supply shock”. “If you’re spending more on fuel, you’ve got less to spend on other things,” he says. In his view the RBA will stop lifting interest rates when unemployment clearly starts to rise. At that point, “the loosening of the labour market will be what drives inflation back towards their target, and they don’t need to tighten further to make that happen”. “But we have to have a downturn in the economy,” Bloxham says. “To get inflation down, there really isn’t another way to do it.” The Australian-20260504-Australian-15-000500646680 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) faces significant challenges in managing monetary policy amid rising inflation and economic uncertainty linked to the Iran war. With inflation at 4.6% and expectations of further interest rate hikes, the RBA is under pressure to balance inflation control with economic growth. Analysts suggest that the RBA may need to continue raising rates despite the risk of pushing the economy into recession.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: The RBA is likely to present various scenarios regarding global energy prices at its upcoming meeting.
Inflation in Australia is currently at 4.6%, with expectations for further rate hikes to combat rising prices.
The RBA's dual mandate to manage inflation and employment is being challenged by stagflationary pressures.
relevance: Understanding the RBA's monetary policy decisions is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as these decisions impact economic forecasts and financial planning.
